reply_text:
Furhman actually pleaded the fifth to "all further questions." This was because it was revealed he lied about using the n word and so he was advised by his lawyer not to answer anything else to protect himself. At that point he hadn't been asked the planting evidence question.

It was only then, when he told the defense what answer he would give to anything else he was asked, did they ask him if he planted evidence. Good move by defense to make him look bad because they knew they were guaranteed to receive a plead-the-fifth answer.

Before the revelation about the n word, Furhman did not plead the fifth regarding questions about planting evidence. He answered a simple "no" when asked if he wiped a glove inside the Bronco, and also said on the stand he was considering a defamation lawsuit against Cochrane and Shapiro for suggesting he planted evidence.

There has never been any evidence showing Furhamn planted anything in this case.

reply_text: @ronm9357there was never any evidence of Fuhrman planting any evidence anywhere. The defense never brought forth any evidence of that and after LAPD investigated those tapes, they found Fuhrman had widely exaggerated his stories for the screenwriter, who hired him as a consultant for her FICTIONAL book and recorded their conversations. Mark Fuhrman arrived at the crime scene after the discovery of the bodies. Fourteen cops were already at the scene. He then went with 3 other detectives to alert OJ Simpson and that’s when he discovered the blood outside AND inside the Bronco. He then did a perimeter check after Kato informed him of the thumping noise and found the other glove. He checked the house and was dismissed from the case that morning. That was the extent of his involvement with this case. When and how would he have planted any evidence? Not saying LAPD didn’t, they certainly did a sloppy job with the investigation and there were some shenanigans but Mark Fuhrman simply had no chance to do it. With his racist past, he was just an easy scapegoat for the defense who relied on the race card to appeal to the majority Black jury.
